original_abstract:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable success in single-label image classification. Translating this success to multi-label scenarios remains a challenge, however, due to the intricate spatial layouts of multiple objects and the relative scarcity of annotated multi-label training data. In this paper, we introduce Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP), a flexible deep learning infrastructure designed specifically for multi-label tasks. 

The HCP framework operates by taking an arbitrary number of object segment hypotheses as input. These hypotheses are processed through a shared CNN, and their output scores are subsequently aggregated via a cross-hypothesis max-pooling layer to generate final multi-label predictions. Key advantages of this architecture include: 1) it eliminates the need for ground-truth bounding box annotations during training; 2) the max-pooling mechanism ensures robustness against noisy or redundant hypotheses; 3) the shared CNN can be effectively pre-trained on massive single-label datasets like ImageNet to compensate for limited multi-label data; and 4) the system naturally produces multi-label outputs without requiring explicit labels for each individual hypothesis. 

Extensive evaluations on the Pascal VOC 2007 and 2012 benchmarks demonstrate that HCP significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. Notably, on the VOC 2012 dataset, HCP achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 84.2% on its own, which rises to a record-breaking 90.3% when integrated with complementary hand-crafted features.
humanized_abstract:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have demonstrated great performance in the single-label image classification problem. However, translating this success to multi-label image classification is not straightforward, because of the complexity of spatial relationships between different objects and limited multi-label training datasets. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning framework, Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP), which can efficiently deal with multi-label image classification tasks.

Traditional region-proposal based approaches require large amounts of bounding box annotations which is very time consuming. Our contribution shows that bounding box annotations can be avoided by developing the HCP model. Our proposed model can support any number of object segment hypotheses. These segment hypotheses go through a shared CNN to which the output score maps are fed to a cross-hypothesis max-pooling layer to obtain multi-label predictions which achieve superior accuracy, robustness and ease of learning than the previous deep learning approaches. Our contributions as outlined above achieve four main advantages. Firstly it has reduced the effort and time required in the training and collection of annotation data for objects in an image which reduces the necessity for large amounts of bounding box annotation information during training time. Secondly it helps to increase accuracy of region proposals by max pooling out the peaks that are suppressed due to the presence of unnecessary object hypotheses. Thirdly the shared architecture of the model and its ability to make use of pre-trained features (pre-trained weights on ImageNet) compensates for lack of large amount of multi-label object segmentation annotations. Fourthly the proposed method is multi-label and object segmentation information of objects present in the images does not require labelling of every object segment hypotheses provided in advance.

We evaluate the performance of our proposed approach (HCP) on the large Pascal VOC 2007 and 2012 datasets and show that it largely outperforms the state-of-the-art results. Specifically, the proposed approach obtains a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 84.2% on the VOC 2012 test set, surpassing the current state-of-the-art performance by a large margin. When combined with hand-crafted complementary features, the mAP further increases to 90.3%, which is a new state-of-the-art result.

document.output:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have demonstrated great performance in the single-label image classification problem. However, translating this success to multi-label image classification is not straightforward, because of the complexity of spatial relationships between different objects and limited multi-label training datasets. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning framework, Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP), which can efficiently deal with multi-label image classification tasks.

Traditional region-proposal based approaches require large amounts of bounding box annotations which is very time consuming. Our contribution shows that bounding box annotations can be avoided by developing the HCP model. Our proposed model can support any number of object segment hypotheses. These segment hypotheses go through a shared CNN to which the output score maps are fed to a cross-hypothesis max-pooling layer to obtain multi-label predictions which achieve superior accuracy, robustness and ease of learning than the previous deep learning approaches. Our contributions as outlined above achieve four main advantages. Firstly it has reduced the effort and time required in the training and collection of annotation data for objects in an image which reduces the necessity for large amounts of bounding box annotation information during training time. Secondly it helps to increase accuracy of region proposals by max pooling out the peaks that are suppressed due to the presence of unnecessary object hypotheses. Thirdly the shared architecture of the model and its ability to make use of pre-trained features (pre-trained weights on ImageNet) compensates for lack of large amount of multi-label object segmentation annotations. Fourthly the proposed method is multi-label and object segmentation information of objects present in the images does not require labelling of every object segment hypotheses provided in advance.

We evaluate the performance of our proposed approach (HCP) on the large Pascal VOC 2007 and 2012 datasets and show that it largely outperforms the state-of-the-art results. Specifically, the proposed approach obtains a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 84.2% on the VOC 2012 test set, surpassing the current state-of-the-art performance by a large margin. When combined with hand-crafted complementary features, the mAP further increases to 90.3%, which is a new state-of-the-art result.
document.input:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable success in single-label image classification. Translating this success to multi-label scenarios remains a challenge, however, due to the intricate spatial layouts of multiple objects and the relative scarcity of annotated multi-label training data. Key advantages of this architecture include: 1) it eliminates the need for ground-truth bounding box annotations during training; 2) the max-pooling mechanism ensures robustness against noisy or redundant hypotheses; 3) the shared CNN can be effectively pre-trained on massive single-label datasets like ImageNet to compensate for limited multi-label data; and 4) the system naturally produces multi-label outputs without requiring explicit labels for each individual hypothesis.

Notably, on the VOC 2012 dataset, HCP achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 84.2% on its own, which rises to a record-breaking 90.3% when integrated with complementary hand-crafted features.
